title: The Gift
description: The Gift — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "礼物" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: Agonistic Exchange
memes.description: A form of gift exchange characterized by competition and rivalry — giving not to create bonds but to defeat rivals through excessive generosity, exemplified by the potlatch of the Pacific Northwest.

memes.label: Alain Caillé
memes.description: French sociologist (1944–), founder of MAUSS (Mouvement Anti-Utilitariste dans les Sciences Sociales), leading figure in the contemporary gift paradigm revival.

memes.label: Alain Testart
memes.description: French anthropologist (1945–2013), author of "Des dons et des dieux" (1993), critical analyst of Mauss's three obligations, specialist in the anthropology of slavery, gift, and hunter-gatherer societies.

memes.label: Alfred Gell
memes.description: British anthropologist (1945–1997), author of Art and Agency (1998), theorist of art objects as extensions of personhood, related to Maussian theories of the person and exchange.

memes.label: Alfred Cort Haddon
memes.description: Alfred Cort Haddon (1855–1940), British anthropologist, leader of the Cambridge Anthropological Expedition to Torres Straits (1898), institutional founder of academic anthropology at Cambridge, teacher of W. H. R. Rivers and A. R. Radcliffe-Brown.

memes.label: L'Année Sociologique
memes.description: The journal and sociological school founded by Émile Durkheim in 1898, which became the institutional foundation of French sociology and anthropology, including the work of Marcel Mauss.

memes.label: Annette Weiner
memes.description: American anthropologist (1933–1997) who revised Mauss's reciprocity theory with the concept of inalienable possessions and the paradox of keeping-while-giving.

memes.label: A.R. Radcliffe-Brown
memes.description: A.R. Radcliffe-Brown (1881–1955), British social anthropologist, founder of structural-functionalism, whose application of Maussian exchange theory to kinship and ritual established gift exchange as a mechanism of social structure maintenance.

memes.label: Arjun Appadurai
memes.description: Indian-American anthropologist (1949–), editor of The Social Life of Things (1986), key theorist of the commodity/gift nexus, globalization, and material culture.

memes.label: Arnold van Gennep
memes.description: Arnold van Gennep (1873–1957), French ethnographer and folklorist, author of Les Rites de Passage (1909), whose theory of ritual passage and cyclical exchange directly influenced Marcel Mauss's analysis of the gift.

memes.label: Barter
memes.description: A form of direct commodity exchange without money, which Mauss argued was not the primitive precursor to market trade but a distinct and historically specific practice.

memes.label: Body Techniques
memes.description: Mauss's concept of the culturally specific ways in which people learn to use their bodies — walking, swimming, eating — demonstrating that even the most "natural" actions are socially patterned.

memes.label: Bronisław Malinowski
memes.description: Bronisław Malinowski (1884–1942), Polish-British anthropologist, pioneer of participant observation, known for his ethnographic studies of the Trobriand Islands and the Kula ring.
aliases: 布罗尼斯拉夫·马林诺夫斯基

memes.label: Ceremonial Exchange
memes.description: A mode of exchange embedded in ritual and social obligation, where goods circulate not for immediate material gain but to create and sustain relationships between individuals, clans, and communities.

memes.label: Charles Gabriel Seligman
memes.description: C.G. Seligman (1873–1940), British anthropologist at LSE, teacher of Malinowski and Evans-Pritchard, pioneering fieldworker in Sudan, Sri Lanka, and the Torres Strait, whose ethnographic work on Melanesia helped lay foundations for Maussian exchange theory.

memes.label: Chris Gregory
memes.description: British economic anthropologist, author of Gifts and Commodities (1982), foundational text in the gift/commodity debate in economic anthropology.

memes.label: Primitive Classification
memes.description: Durkheim and Mauss's theory that systems of logical classification (categories, taxonomies) are derived from social organization, not from individual cognition.

memes.label: Claude Lévi-Strauss
memes.description: French anthropologist (1908–2009), founder of structural anthropology, whose theory of kinship as reciprocal exchange built directly on Mauss's The Gift.

memes.label: Clifford Geertz
memes.description: Clifford Geertz (1926–2006), American cultural anthropologist, author of "The Interpretation of Cultures" (1973), pioneer of symbolic and interpretive anthropology, known for the method of "thick description" and the analysis of culture as webs of meaning.

memes.label: Collective Representation
memes.description: Durkheim's concept of shared mental representations that exist independently of individual minds — the social foundation of categories, beliefs, and symbolic systems that Mauss applied to gift exchange.

memes.label: Collège de France
memes.description: France's most prestigious research institution, founded 1530, where Marcel Mauss was elected to the Chair of Sociology in 1931 and delivered his final major lecture series.

memes.label: Counter-Gift
memes.description: The return gift (contre-don) — the third moment in Mauss's triple obligation, which closes the gift cycle while opening a new one, creating the perpetual circulation that binds societies.

memes.label: David Cheal
memes.description: David Cheal, contemporary Canadian sociologist, author of The Gift Economy (1988), who analyzed modern gift practices (Christmas, weddings, birthdays) through the Maussian framework, theorizing the moral economy of small gifts in capitalist societies.

memes.label: David Graeber
memes.description: American anthropologist and activist (1961–2020), known for his Maussian theory of value, Debt: The First 5,000 Years, and his role in the Occupy movement.

memes.label: École Pratique des Hautes Études
memes.description: French academic institution founded in 1868, where Marcel Mauss held a chair in the "History of Religions of Uncivilized Peoples" and trained a generation of French anthropologists.

memes.label: Edmund Leach
memes.description: British social anthropologist (1910–1989), student of Malinowski, author of "Political Systems of Highland Burma" (1954), key figure in introducing French structuralism to British anthropology.

memes.label: Edward Sapir
memes.description: Edward Sapir (1884–1939), American anthropological linguist, student of Franz Boas, pioneer of language-culture studies, known for the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis and the linguistic classification of Native American languages.

memes.label: Edward Burnett Tylor
memes.description: Edward Burnett Tylor (1832–1917), British anthropologist, author of "Primitive Culture" (1871), foundational figure of cultural evolutionism, known for the concept of "survivals" and the animistic theory of religion.

memes.label: E.E. Evans-Pritchard
memes.description: E.E. Evans-Pritchard (1902–1973), British social anthropologist, author of The Nuer (1940) and Witchcraft, Oracles and Magic among the Azande (1937), who applied Maussian total social fact to segmentary lineage systems and gift-sacrifice relations in African political anthropology.

memes.label: Elman Service
memes.description: Elman Service (1915–1996), American anthropologist, neo-evolutionist, known for the band–tribe–chiefdom–state typology of political evolution and contributions to cultural evolution theory alongside Marshall Sahlins.

memes.label: Elsdon Best
memes.description: New Zealand ethnographer (1856–1931); recorded Māori traditions, including Tamati Ranapiri's explanation of the hau of taonga — the account that became the centrepiece of Mauss's theory of gift reciprocity.

memes.label: Embeddedness
memes.description: Karl Polanyi's principle that pre-modern economies are "embedded" in social relations rather than operating as autonomous market systems — a theoretical framework complementary to Mauss's analysis of the gift.

memes.label: Émile Durkheim
memes.description: French sociologist (1858–1917), founder of the Année Sociologique school and mentor to Marcel Mauss, who established sociology as an academic discipline in France.

memes.label: Eric Wolf
memes.description: Eric Wolf (1923–1999), Austrian-American anthropologist, known for Europe and the People Without History (1982), applying world-systems theory to anthropology, and analyzing how global commodity chains transform local gift economies.

memes.label: Expenditure
memes.description: Georges Bataille's concept of non-productive expenditure (dépense) — the ritual destruction of wealth as a form of sovereignty, directly linked to Mauss's analysis of potlatch.

memes.label: Franz Boas
memes.description: German-American anthropologist (1858–1942), founder of American cultural anthropology; principal ethnographer of the Kwakiutl potlatch and teacher of Ruth Benedict and Margaret Mead.

memes.label: Frederick Damon
memes.description: American anthropologist, ethnographer of the kula ring, author of "From Muyuw to the Trobriands" (1990), specialist in Maussian exchange systems in Melanesia.

memes.label: Fredrik Barth
memes.description: Fredrik Barth (1928–2016), Norwegian social anthropologist, trained by Edmund Leach and the Manchester School, known for transactionalist theory of social organization and the situational analysis of ethnicity in "Ethnic Groups and Boundaries" (1969).

memes.label: George Dalton
memes.description: George Dalton (1926–1991), American economic anthropologist, Karl Polanyi's student and key substantivist school figure, editor of "Primitive, Archaic and Modern Economies" (1968).

memes.label: Georges Bataille
memes.description: Georges Bataille (1897–1962), French philosopher and writer, theorist of expenditure (dépense) and the accursed share, member of the Collège de Sociologie.

memes.label: Georges Davy
memes.description: French sociologist (1883–1976), member of the Durkheimian school, who collaborated with Mauss on the study of archaic forms of contract in La Foi Jurée (1922).

memes.label: Gift Economy
memes.description: A mode of exchange where goods and services are given without explicit agreement for immediate or future return, as theorized by Marcel Mauss in The Gift.

memes.label: Gilbert Ryle
memes.description: Gilbert Ryle (1900–1976), British philosopher, leading figure of Oxford ordinary-language philosophy, known for The Concept of Mind (1949), the distinction between "knowing-how" and "knowing-that," and the concept of "thick description" later appropriated by Clifford Geertz.

memes.label: Godfrey Lienhardt
memes.description: Godfrey Lienhardt (1921–1993), British anthropologist, Evans-Pritchard's successor at Oxford, author of "Divinity and Experience: The Religion of the Dinka" (1961), whose analysis of sacrifice, personhood, and ritual symbolism extended the Oxford tradition of Nilotic studies.

memes.label: Gregory Bateson
memes.description: Gregory Bateson (1904–1980), British anthropologist and cybernetic theorist, author of "Naven" (1936), known for schismogenesis theory, the double bind hypothesis, and "Steps to an Ecology of Mind" (1972).

memes.label: Habitus
memes.description: Mauss's concept of socially acquired bodily dispositions and techniques, later developed by Bourdieu into a theory of structured social practice.

memes.label: Hau (spirit of the gift)
memes.description: The Māori concept of the "spirit of the gift" — a spiritual essence residing in the gift-object that compels its eventual return to the original giver, as interpreted by Marcel Mauss.

memes.label: Henri Hubert
memes.description: Henri Hubert (1872–1927), French archaeologist and sociologist, close collaborator of Marcel Mauss, co-author of foundational works on sacrifice and magic.

memes.label: Igor Kopytoff
memes.description: Russian-born American anthropologist, author of "The Cultural Biography of Things" (1986), pioneer of commoditization and singularization theory within economic anthropology.

memes.label: Inalienable Possessions
memes.description: Annette Weiner's concept that certain objects must be kept while being given — a revision of Mauss's theory of reciprocity that emphasizes the paradox of "keeping-while-giving."

memes.label: Institut d'Ethnologie
memes.description: The Institute of Ethnology at the University of Paris, co-founded in 1925 by Marcel Mauss, Lucien Lévy-Bruhl, and Paul Rivet, which trained a generation of French ethnologists.

memes.label: Jack Goody
memes.description: Jack Goody (1919–2015), British social anthropologist, Cambridge professor, student of Evans-Pritchard, whose comparative studies of kinship, inheritance, and literacy transformed the anthropology of property transmission and the consequences of writing.

memes.label: Jacques Derrida
memes.description: French philosopher (1930–2004), founder of deconstruction, author of Given Time (1991), a major philosophical engagement with Mauss's theory of the gift.

memes.label: Jacques Godbout
memes.description: Quebec sociologist (1939–), co-author with Alain Caillé of "L'esprit du don" (The Spirit of the Gift, 1992), a foundational text of the contemporary gift paradigm examining gift practices in modern societies.

memes.label: James Carrier
memes.description: "American economic anthropologist, editor of "Gifts and Commodities: Exchange and Western Capitalism Since 1700" (1995), central figure in the gift/commodity debate in economic anthropology."

memes.label: James Clifford
memes.description: James Clifford (b. 1945), American historian of anthropology and cultural critic, editor of Writing Culture (1986), known for reflexive critique of ethnographic writing and scholarship on the Collège de Sociologie, Maussian surrealism, and the politics of cultural representation.

memes.label: James George Frazer
memes.description: J.G. Frazer (1854–1941), Scottish social anthropologist, author of The Golden Bough (1890), first chair of Social Anthropology at Cambridge, whose comparative study of magic, religion, and myth provided the ethnographic foundation that Mauss reworked into gift and sacrifice theory.

memes.label: John Layard
memes.description: John Layard (1891–1974), British anthropologist and Jungian psychotherapist, pioneering ethnographer of Malakula (Vanuatu), student of A. C. Haddon at Cambridge, contributor to early kula ring studies and the anthropology of ritual exchange in Melanesia.

memes.label: Jonathan Parry
memes.description: British anthropologist, author of "The Gift, the Indian Gift, and the 'Indian Gift'" (1986), key contributor to comparative gift theory and the sociology of exchange in South Asia.

memes.label: Julian Steward
memes.description: Julian Steward (1902–1972), American anthropologist, founder of cultural ecology, leading figure of neoevolutionism alongside Leslie White, known for the concept of the "culture core" and multilinear evolution.

memes.label: Karl Polanyi
memes.description: Hungarian economic historian and anthropologist (1886–1964), founder of substantivist economic anthropology; author of The Great Transformation (1944).

memes.label: Keith Hart
memes.description: "British anthropologist (b. 1943), pioneer of the informal economy concept, author of The Memory Bank (2000), advocate of a Maussian human economy paradigm."

memes.label: Kula Exchange
memes.description: A ceremonial exchange system in the Massim region of Papua New Guinea, documented by Bronisław Malinowski, in which shell valuables circulate between island communities in a closed ring.

memes.label: Leslie White
memes.description: Leslie White (1900–1975), American anthropologist, leading figure of neoevolutionism, known for the energy theory of cultural evolution — "culture advances as the amount of energy harnessed per capita per year increases" — and as the teacher of Marshall Sahlins at the University of Michigan.

memes.label: Lewis Henry Morgan
memes.description: Lewis Henry Morgan (1818–1881), American anthropologist, foundational figure of 19th-century cultural evolutionism, author of "Ancient Society" (1877), pioneer of kinship studies and Iroquois ethnography.

memes.label: Lewis Hyde
memes.description: American writer and cultural critic (1945–), author of The Gift: Creativity and the Artist in the Modern World (1983), which popularized Maussian gift theory in arts and literature.

memes.label: Louis Dumont
memes.description: French anthropologist (1911–1998), student of Mauss, known for his study of the Indian caste system, Homo Hierarchicus (1966), and the comparative sociology of individualism.

memes.label: Louis Gernet
memes.description: Louis Gernet (1882–1962), French classicist and sociologist, student of Mauss and Durkheim, whose application of Maussian gift theory to ancient Greek law and religion founded the anthropological study of classical antiquity.

memes.label: Lucien Lévy-Bruhl
memes.description: Lucien Lévy-Bruhl (1857–1939), French philosopher and anthropologist, author of La Mentalité primitive (1922), colleague of Marcel Mauss and Émile Durkheim.

memes.label: Magic
memes.description: Mauss and Hubert's sociological theory of magic as a social phenomenon distinct from religion, based on the collective belief in magical efficacy (mana) and the social position of the magician.

memes.label: Mana
memes.description: A Melanesian and Polynesian concept of impersonal supernatural power or efficacy that resides in persons, objects, and places — a parallel concept to the Māori hau that Mauss used to explain the spiritual compulsion behind gift exchange.

memes.label: Marcel Griaule
memes.description: Marcel Griaule (1898–1956), French ethnographer, Mauss student at the Institut d'Ethnologie, leader of the Dakar–Djibouti expedition (1931–1933), whose Dogon cosmology studies (Dieu d'eau, 1948) pioneered collaborative ethnographic fieldwork in the Maussian tradition.

memes.label: Marcel Mauss
memes.description: Marcel Mauss (1872–1950), French sociologist and anthropologist, author of The Gift (Essai sur le don), nephew and collaborator of Émile Durkheim.

memes.label: Margaret Mead
memes.description: Margaret Mead (1901–1978), American cultural anthropologist, student of Franz Boas, whose Melanesian ethnographies of exchange, gender, and socialization made anthropology accessible to a global public and established the culture-and-personality school.

memes.label: Marilyn Strathern
memes.description: British anthropologist (1941–), author of The Gender of the Gift (1988), foundational figure in feminist anthropology and Melanesian exchange studies.

memes.label: Mark Osteen
memes.description: "American literary scholar, editor of "The Question of the Gift: Essays Across Disciplines" (2002), a major interdisciplinary anthology that brought together anthropology, economics, philosophy, and literary studies on the gift."

memes.label: Marshall Sahlins
memes.description: American anthropologist (1930–2021); major theorist of reciprocity and gift exchange, author of Stone Age Economics (1972) and key critic of Mauss's interpretation of Māori hau.

memes.label: Marvin Harris
memes.description: Marvin Harris (1927–2001), American anthropologist, founder of cultural materialism, known for infrastructural determinism and materialist analyses of sacred cattle, potlatch warfare, and food taboos in works such as Cows, Pigs, Wars, and Witches (1974).

memes.label: Mary Douglas
memes.description: Mary Douglas (1921–2007), British social anthropologist, author of Purity and Danger (1966) and The World of Goods (1979), applied Maussian classification and gift theory to modern consumer societies.

memes.label: Maurice Bloch
memes.description: British-French anthropologist (b. 1939), co-editor with Jonathan Parry of "Money and the Morality of Exchange" (1989), major contributor to Marxist economic anthropology and gift/money theory.

memes.label: Maurice Godelier
memes.description: French anthropologist (b. 1934), director of studies at EHESS, who directly engaged Mauss's theory of the gift in The Enigma of the Gift (1996).

memes.label: Maurice Halbwachs
memes.description: Maurice Halbwachs (1877–1945), French sociologist of the Durkheim school, author of Les Cadres sociaux de la mémoire (1925), whose theory of collective memory extended Durkheimian collective representations into the study of how social groups remember, and who died at Buchenwald.

memes.label: Maurice Leenhardt
memes.description: Maurice Leenhardt (1878–1954), French missionary-ethnographer, author of "Do Kamo: Person and Myth in the Melanesian World" (1947), whose New Caledonia ethnography directly influenced Mauss's 1938 lecture on the category of the person.

memes.label: Max Gluckman
memes.description: Max Gluckman (1911–1982), South African-British social anthropologist, founder of the Manchester School, known for extending social anthropology beyond structural-functionalism through the analysis of conflict, social process, and ritual in political life.

memes.label: Melanesia
memes.description: A subregion of Oceania extending from Papua New Guinea to Fiji, home to the Trobriand Islands and the Kula exchange system documented by Malinowski and Mauss.

memes.label: Meyer Fortes
memes.description: Meyer Fortes (1906–1983), South African-British social anthropologist, co-editor of African Political Systems (1940), applied Maussian exchange and reciprocity theory to Tallensi kinship and African political anthropology.

memes.label: Michel Leiris
memes.description: Michel Leiris (1901–1990), French surrealist writer and ethnographer, co-founder of the Collège de Sociologie with Bataille and Caillois, who applied Maussian sacred theory to ethnographic writing and autobiography in L'Afrique fantôme (1934).

memes.label: Moral Economy
memes.description: E.P. Thompson and James C. Scott's concept that economic behaviour is governed by moral norms of justice and subsistence rights, not merely market rationality — a thesis with deep affinities to Maussian gift theory.

memes.label: Mwali and Soulava
memes.description: The two ceremonial valuables of the Kula ring — mwali (white shell armbands) and soulava (red shell necklaces) — that circulate in opposite directions, binding exchange partners across the Massim region.

memes.label: Nancy Munn
memes.description: American anthropologist (1931–2020), author of The Fame of Gawa (1986), key theorist of value transformation in the Kula exchange of Melanesia.

memes.label: Nurit Bird-David
memes.description: Israeli anthropologist, known for "The Giving Environment" (1990) and her paradigm-shifting analysis of hunter-gatherer sharing as a gift-based economic model distinct from both reciprocity and exchange.

memes.label: Obligation
memes.description: Mauss's concept of the triple obligation — to give, to receive, and to reciprocate — as the fundamental mechanism binding societies together through gift exchange.

memes.label: Pacific Northwest Coast
memes.description: A cultural region of North America stretching from southern Alaska to northern California, home to indigenous peoples known for the potlatch ceremony, totem poles, and complex gift-giving traditions.

memes.label: Papua New Guinea
memes.description: The largest nation in Melanesia, occupying the eastern half of the island of New Guinea, home to the Trobriand Islands and over 800 indigenous languages.

memes.label: Paul Bohannan
memes.description: Paul Bohannan (1920–2007), American anthropologist, developed Tiv spheres of exchange theory, a foundational concept in substantivist economic anthropology.

memes.label: Paul Rivet
memes.description: Paul Rivet (1876–1958), French ethnologist and anthropologist, co-founder of the Musée de l'Homme and the Institut d'Ethnologie, early theorist of American migration routes.

memes.label: Personne
memes.description: Mauss's concept of the person (personne) as a historically variable social category — the idea that the notion of the individual self is not a human universal but a cultural and historical construct.

memes.label: Pierre Bourdieu
memes.description: French sociologist and anthropologist (1930–2002); developed practice theory (habitus/field/capital) drawing significantly on Maussian gift theory and Lévi-Straussian structuralism.

memes.label: Potlatch
memes.description: A ceremonial feast and gift-giving institution of the indigenous peoples of the Pacific Northwest Coast, involving competitive distribution and sometimes destruction of wealth to assert status and rank.

memes.label: Raymond Firth
memes.description: New Zealand anthropologist (1901–2002), Malinowski's student, foundational figure in economic anthropology, author of studies on Maori gift exchange and Polynesian economy.

memes.label: Reciprocity (anthropology)
memes.description: The moral principle of mutual obligation in gift exchange — to give, to receive, and to reciprocate — that Mauss identified as the foundation of pre-market economic systems.

memes.label: Reo Fortune
memes.description: Reo Fortune (1903–1979), New Zealand-born anthropologist, author of "Sorcerers of Dobu" (1932), key Mauss source for Dobu Island kula exchange data.

memes.label: Richard Thurnwald
memes.description: Richard Thurnwald (1869–1954), German ethnologist and sociologist who coined "reciprocity" (Gegenseitigkeit) as an analytical concept, directly cited by Marcel Mauss in The Gift.

memes.label: Robert Hertz
memes.description: Robert Hertz (1881–1915), French sociologist and anthropologist of the Année Sociologique school, author of foundational studies on death, sin, and the pre-eminence of the right hand.

memes.label: Roger Caillois
memes.description: Roger Caillois (1913–1978), French sociologist and literary critic, co-founder of the Collège de Sociologie with Georges Bataille, author of "Man and the Sacred" (1939).

memes.label: Roy Rappaport
memes.description: Roy Rappaport (1926–1997), American ecological anthropologist, known for ritual regulation theory and the cybernetic analysis of Maring pig-slaughter cycles in Pigs for the Ancestors (1968).

memes.label: Ruth Benedict
memes.description: Ruth Benedict (1887–1948), American cultural anthropologist, student of Franz Boas, author of "Patterns of Culture" (1934), whose analysis of potlatch as agonistic exchange and culture as "personality writ large" shaped the culture-and-personality school.

memes.label: Sacrifice
memes.description: Hubert and Mauss's theory of sacrifice as a ritual mechanism for mediating between the sacred and profane through the consecration and destruction of a victim.

memes.label: Structuralism (anthropology)
memes.description: An anthropological methodology, pioneered by Claude Lévi-Strauss, that analyzes cultural phenomena — including gift exchange — as manifestations of underlying, unconscious mental structures of binary opposition and reciprocal exchange.

memes.label: Talcott Parsons
memes.description: Talcott Parsons (1902–1979), American sociologist, founder of structural functionalism, known for the theory of social action, the AGIL schema, and the concept of generalized symbolic media of exchange including money, power, influence, and value-commitments.

memes.label: Taonga
memes.description: A Māori concept referring to treasures, heirlooms, and culturally significant possessions that carry the spiritual essence (hau) of their givers and ancestors.

memes.label: Total Prestation
memes.description: Marcel Mauss's concept of exchanges that are simultaneously legal, economic, religious, aesthetic, and social — a "total social fact" where entire groups engage in reciprocal obligations.

memes.label: Trobriand Islands
memes.description: A group of coral islands in the Solomon Sea, part of Papua New Guinea, known in anthropology as the site of Bronisław Malinowski's fieldwork and the Kula exchange system.

memes.label: Victor Turner
memes.description: Victor Turner (1920–1983), British anthropologist, author of "The Ritual Process" (1969), who extended van Gennep's rites of passage into a general theory of liminality, communitas, and symbolic action central to the study of ritual exchange.

memes.label: W.H.R. Rivers
memes.description: W.H.R. Rivers (1864–1922), British anthropologist, physician, and psychologist, leader of the Torres Straits Expedition (1898), whose work on kinship classification, diffusion, and social organization laid groundwork for British social anthropology and influenced Radcliffe-Brown.
